attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage
75	
76	The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.
77	
78	Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.
79	
80	**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.
81	
82	Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.
83	
84	Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.
85	
86	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
87	
88	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
89	
90	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
91	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
92	
93	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
94	
95	Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.
96	
97	**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.
98	
99	## Suite-level fail-fast
100	
101	Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.
102	
103	## No-teardown decision
104	
105	The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:
106	
107	- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
108	- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
109	- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
110	- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.
111	
112	Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).
113	
114	## Operator scope
115	
116	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.
117	
118	## Coupling to ₣A_
119	
120	Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.
121	
122	## Locked design decisions
123	
124	- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
125	- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
126	- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.
127	
128	## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh
129	
130	Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.
131	
132	**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.
133	
134	**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):
135	
136	- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
137	- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
138	- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
139	- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.
140	
141	Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.
142	
143	Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.
144	
145	## References
146	
147	- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
148	- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
149	- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
150	- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
151	- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)
152	
153	# jjezs_pace ₢BBABJ
154	
155	## Character
156	
157	Mechanical: mint a thin BCG CLI + two tabtargets for the `rbfh` module landed in `₢BBABI`, then add a ~9-case theurge fixture in the fast tier for regression coverage of the Dockerfile hygiene contract. Patterns follow `regime-validation` for both reach (path-mode tabtarget per synthetic case, single all-vessels case with bash-side iteration) and shape (inline Rust string constants for synthetic Dockerfile bodies, mirroring `rbtdrf_rv_*` setup strings).
158	
159	## Mint
160	
161	- `Tools/rbk/rbfh_cli.sh` — BCG CLI paired with the existing `rbfh_FoundryHygiene.sh` library module (sourced therein). Kindle chain loads buc + rbfc (FoundryCore — needed for vessel resolution) + rbrr regime (for `RBRR_VESSEL_DIR`) + rbrv regime + rbfh. No credentials, no GCP machinery.
162	- Public functions:
163	  - `rbfh_check <dockerfile-path>` — direct path check; wraps `rbfh_dockerfile_check` after sentinel.
164	  - `rbfh_check_vessel <vessel-sigil-or-path>` — resolves via `zrbfc_resolve_vessel`, sources vessel rbrv.env, asserts conjure-mode (refuses bind/graft with operator-actionable message), runs check on `RBRV_CONJURE_DOCKERFILE`. No-arg → list available vessels and die helpfully (match `rbfd_build`'s pattern at `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:1178`).
165	- Tabtargets (both param1 channel; lowercase `h` since hygiene is read-only/local, paralleling `rbw-fpf`/`rbw-fpc`):
166	  - `tt/rbw-fhc.HygieneCheck.sh` → `rbfh_check`
167	  - `tt/rbw-fhv.HygieneCheckVessel.sh` → `rbfh_check_vessel`
168	- Zipper entries in `rbz_zipper.sh`: `RBZ_HYGIENE_CHECK` and `RBZ_HYGIENE_CHECK_VESSEL`.
169	
170	## Fixture
171	
172	- New theurge fixture `dockerfile-hygiene` defined in `rbtdrf_fast.rs`, registered in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`, assigned to the fast suite.
173	- Synthetic cases (~8): Dockerfile bodies as inline Rust `&'static str` constants; each case writes the body to a temp file in the case's `dir`, invokes `tt/rbw-fhc <tempfile>` via `rbtdrf_run_tt`, asserts verdict. Coverage:
174	  - `dh_accept_parameterized` (positive — `FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_1}`)
175	  - `dh_accept_scratch` (positive — `FROM scratch`)
176	  - `dh_accept_multistage_as` (positive — `AS builder` coda admissible)
177	  - `dh_accept_empty` (positive — empty file passes silently)
178	  - `dh_accept_comments_only` (positive — column-0 `#` lines only)
179	  - `dh_reject_hardcoded_literal` (negative — `FROM python:3.12-slim`)
180	  - `dh_reject_tab_in_from` (negative — tab anywhere on FROM line)
181	  - `dh_reject_trailing_backslash` (negative — line ends with `\`)
182	- One all-vessels case `dh_all_vessels_pass`: bash script iterates `${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/*` (mirroring `rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels` at `rbtdrf_fast.rs:967`), sources each `rbrv.env` in a subshell to read `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`, skips non-conjure modes, invokes `tt/rbw-fhv <sigil>` per conjure vessel, fails on any non-zero. No hardcoded vessel paths in theurge — discovery flows through the regime's own pointer.
183	
184	## Touches
185	
186	- CLAUDE.md File Acronym Mappings: extend the RBFH entry to mention the CLI counterpart, or add a sibling `rbfh_cli.sh` line.
187	- Regenerate `rbk-claude-tabtarget-context.md` via `tt/rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh` so the two new tabtargets appear in the Foundry table.
188	- No spec edits required: `RBSFH-dockerfile_hygiene.adoc` describes the rule itself, not the test surface. `RBSAK`/`RBSAC` references to `{rbsfh_dockerfile_hygiene}` already landed in `₢BBABI`.
189	
190	## Verification
191	
192	- `bash -n` clean on `rbfh_cli.sh` and any touched modules.
193	- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` green (case count grows ~98 → ~107).
194	- Sanity-check: temporarily flip one synthetic case's `expect_ok` flag, confirm the fixture reports the case as failed (verdict assertion is wired correctly), then revert.
195	
196	## Out of scope
197	
198	- Handbook prose for the new tabtargets — operators discover via `ls tt/` plus context. Chivvy a doc itch if drift becomes noticeable.
199	- Spec subdoc edits to RBSFH for the test surface — fixture is implementation detail, not contract.
200	- Decomposing `rbfh_check_vessel`'s no-arg list-and-die into a separate `rbfh_list_vessels` function — premature factoring.
201	
202	## Silks
203	
204	Current proposal `rbfh-cli-and-theurge-fixture` fits if `cli` and `fixture` both feel load-bearing at mount time. If just one (probably the fixture) ends up doing most of the work, consider relabeling.

# jjezs_paddock ₣BB

## Context

Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.

This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.

## Single operator prerequisite chain

Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.

## Entry contract — load-bearing

`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:

- Working tree clean
- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files

Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.

## Failure mode contract

Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.

## Release-branch execution contract

Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.

**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.

**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.

**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.

Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.

## Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

## Gauntlet test suite — design

`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.

**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.

**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).

**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:

1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)

No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.

## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage

The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.

Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.

**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.

Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.

Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.

## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing

Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:

- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.

**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.

Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.

**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.

## Suite-level fail-fast

Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.

## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions

- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.

## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh

Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.

Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.

Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.

## References

- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

# jjezs_pace ₢BBABJ

## Character

Mechanical: mint a thin BCG CLI + two tabtargets for the `rbfh` module landed in `₢BBABI`, then add a ~9-case theurge fixture in the fast tier for regression coverage of the Dockerfile hygiene contract. Patterns follow `regime-validation` for both reach (path-mode tabtarget per synthetic case, single all-vessels case with bash-side iteration) and shape (inline Rust string constants for synthetic Dockerfile bodies, mirroring `rbtdrf_rv_*` setup strings).

## Mint

- `Tools/rbk/rbfh_cli.sh` — BCG CLI paired with the existing `rbfh_FoundryHygiene.sh` library module (sourced therein). Kindle chain loads buc + rbfc (FoundryCore — needed for vessel resolution) + rbrr regime (for `RBRR_VESSEL_DIR`) + rbrv regime + rbfh. No credentials, no GCP machinery.
- Public functions:
  - `rbfh_check <dockerfile-path>` — direct path check; wraps `rbfh_dockerfile_check` after sentinel.
  - `rbfh_check_vessel <vessel-sigil-or-path>` — resolves via `zrbfc_resolve_vessel`, sources vessel rbrv.env, asserts conjure-mode (refuses bind/graft with operator-actionable message), runs check on `RBRV_CONJURE_DOCKERFILE`. No-arg → list available vessels and die helpfully (match `rbfd_build`'s pattern at `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:1178`).
- Tabtargets (both param1 channel; lowercase `h` since hygiene is read-only/local, paralleling `rbw-fpf`/`rbw-fpc`):
  - `tt/rbw-fhc.HygieneCheck.sh` → `rbfh_check`
  - `tt/rbw-fhv.HygieneCheckVessel.sh` → `rbfh_check_vessel`
- Zipper entries in `rbz_zipper.sh`: `RBZ_HYGIENE_CHECK` and `RBZ_HYGIENE_CHECK_VESSEL`.

## Fixture

- New theurge fixture `dockerfile-hygiene` defined in `rbtdrf_fast.rs`, registered in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`, assigned to the fast suite.
- Synthetic cases (~8): Dockerfile bodies as inline Rust `&'static str` constants; each case writes the body to a temp file in the case's `dir`, invokes `tt/rbw-fhc <tempfile>` via `rbtdrf_run_tt`, asserts verdict. Coverage:
  - `dh_accept_parameterized` (positive — `FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_1}`)
  - `dh_accept_scratch` (positive — `FROM scratch`)
  - `dh_accept_multistage_as` (positive — `AS builder` coda admissible)
  - `dh_accept_empty` (positive — empty file passes silently)
  - `dh_accept_comments_only` (positive — column-0 `#` lines only)
  - `dh_reject_hardcoded_literal` (negative — `FROM python:3.12-slim`)
  - `dh_reject_tab_in_from` (negative — tab anywhere on FROM line)
  - `dh_reject_trailing_backslash` (negative — line ends with `\`)
- One all-vessels case `dh_all_vessels_pass`: bash script iterates `${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/*` (mirroring `rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels` at `rbtdrf_fast.rs:967`), sources each `rbrv.env` in a subshell to read `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`, skips non-conjure modes, invokes `tt/rbw-fhv <sigil>` per conjure vessel, fails on any non-zero. No hardcoded vessel paths in theurge — discovery flows through the regime's own pointer.

## Touches

- CLAUDE.md File Acronym Mappings: extend the RBFH entry to mention the CLI counterpart, or add a sibling `rbfh_cli.sh` line.
- Regenerate `rbk-claude-tabtarget-context.md` via `tt/rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh` so the two new tabtargets appear in the Foundry table.
- No spec edits required: `RBSFH-dockerfile_hygiene.adoc` describes the rule itself, not the test surface. `RBSAK`/`RBSAC` references to `{rbsfh_dockerfile_hygiene}` already landed in `₢BBABI`.

## Verification

- `bash -n` clean on `rbfh_cli.sh` and any touched modules.
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` green (case count grows ~98 → ~107).
- Sanity-check: temporarily flip one synthetic case's `expect_ok` flag, confirm the fixture reports the case as failed (verdict assertion is wired correctly), then revert.

## Out of scope

- Handbook prose for the new tabtargets — operators discover via `ls tt/` plus context. Chivvy a doc itch if drift becomes noticeable.
- Spec subdoc edits to RBSFH for the test surface — fixture is implementation detail, not contract.
- Decomposing `rbfh_check_vessel`'s no-arg list-and-die into a separate `rbfh_list_vessels` function — premature factoring.

## Silks

Current proposal `rbfh-cli-and-theurge-fixture` fits if `cli` and `fixture` both feel load-bearing at mount time. If just one (probably the fixture) ends up doing most of the work, consider relabeling.

content.text:
## Pace goal

Mint a thin `rbfh` CLI (paired with the existing `rbfh_FoundryHygiene.sh` library) plus two `tt/rbw-fh*` tabtargets, then add a ~9-case `dockerfile-hygiene` theurge fixture in the fast tier to regression-lock the Dockerfile hygiene contract that landed in `₢BBABI`.

## Heat context

**₣BB** rbk-15-mvp-release-qualification — building `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine as the third release tier whose entry contract refuses to run unless marshal-zero was just committed. Recent work landed `rbfh_FoundryHygiene.sh` (`₢BBABI`) closing the hardcoded-FROM bypass with the three-rule check wired into both kludge and conjure paths. This pace is the cantled regression-protection follow-on.

## Docket summary

**Mint** (CLI + tabtargets + zipper):
- `Tools/rbk/rbfh_cli.sh` — BCG CLI, kindle: buc + rbfc + rbrr + rbrv + rbfh; no credentials/GCP
- `rbfh_check <path>` — direct path check
- `rbfh_check_vessel <sigil-or-path>` — resolves vessel, asserts conjure-mode, no-arg lists vessels (matches `rbfd_build:1178` pattern)
- `tt/rbw-fhc.HygieneCheck.sh` + `tt/rbw-fhv.HygieneCheckVessel.sh` (both param1, lowercase `h` paralleling `rbw-fpf/fpc`)
- Zipper: `RBZ_HYGIENE_CHECK`, `RBZ_HYGIENE_CHECK_VESSEL`

**Fixture** (`dockerfile-hygiene` in `rbtdrf_fast.rs`):
- 8 synthetic cases (5 accept, 3 reject) — Dockerfile bodies as inline `&'static str`, write to case `dir`, invoke `tt/rbw-fhc <tempfile>` via `rbtdrf_run_tt`
- 1 all-vessels case — bash iterates `${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/*`, sources each rbrv.env, skips non-conjure, invokes `tt/rbw-fhv <sigil>` (mirrors `rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels:967`)

**Touches**: CLAUDE.md acronym mapping extension, regenerate tabtarget context via `rbw-MG`; no spec edits required.

**Verification**: bash -n; fast suite ~98 → ~107 green; expect-flip sanity check on one case.

## Name assessment

Silks `rbfh-cli-and-theurge-fixture` — docket explicitly flags the rename trigger ("if just one ends up doing most of the work"). Both halves are load-bearing as drafted: the CLI mints new public surface (functions, two tabtargets, two zipper entries) and the fixture exercises it. Keep as-is unless one collapses during work.

## Execution strategy

**Sequential sonnet** — mechanical pattern-matching across multiple files with order dependencies (CLI must exist before fixture invokes it via tabtarget). Pattern templates are nearby and explicit (regime-validation for fixture shape, rbfc/rbfd for CLI shape). No architectural judgment calls.

Approach:
1. Mint `rbfh_cli.sh` + tabtargets + zipper entries
2. `rbw-MG` to regenerate tabtarget context
3. CLAUDE.md acronym edit
4. Author `dockerfile-hygiene` fixture in `rbtdrf_fast.rs`, register in manifest, assign to fast suite
5. Fast suite green + expect-flip sanity check
6. `bash -n` sweep on touched modules

Ready to proceed?

No — the docket has a gap. Here's the empirical pattern at `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh:48-69`:

```
ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=("enrollment-validation" "regime-validation" "regime-smoke" "handbook-render")
ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" ...)
ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" ...)
ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" ...)
ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    ...
)
```

Service/crucible/complete **spread** `ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]` — adding `dockerfile-hygiene` to fast propagates to them for free. But **gauntlet hand-enumerates** its members; it cherry-picks the fast-tier fixtures it wants (`regime-validation`, `regime-smoke` are explicit). New fast fixtures do NOT flow into gauntlet automatically.

The docket says only "assigned to the fast suite" (line 172) and "case count grows ~98 → ~107" (line 193). It is silent on gauntlet membership.

**My read**: `dockerfile-hygiene` should be in gauntlet. It's state-indifferent and sits naturally alongside `regime-validation`/`regime-smoke` in the post-§3 slot (the all-vessels case needs `${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}` populated). The hygiene contract is exactly the kind of thing the release gate should re-verify — silent first-build assumption is the bug class `rbw-tP` exists to catch.

Two missing items in the docket:
1. **Mint section**: add `dockerfile-hygiene` to `ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET` between `regime-smoke` and `hallmark-lifecycle` (post-§3 placement)
2. **Verification section**: a quick grep-confirm of gauntlet membership (running the full gauntlet itself is cost-prohibitive at slate-time; the membership-by-inspection check is enough)

Want me to reslate the pace with the gauntlet wiring spelled in, or do you want to think about whether `dockerfile-hygiene` belongs in gauntlet at all first?

Belief is **incorrect**. Empirical at `Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh:188-200`:

```
rbq_qualify_pristine() {
  zrbq_sentinel
  buc_step "Running pristine qualification (gauntlet test suite)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh"
  buc_step "Pristine qualification passed"
}
```

`rbw-tP` runs gauntlet directly — no fast phase. Compare `rbq_qualify_release` (`rbq_Qualify.sh:160-186`) which has phases including a `complete` suite run; complete spreads fast so it picks up everything fast-tier transitively.

Gauntlet is hand-enumerated and does NOT spread `ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]`. Adding `dockerfile-hygiene` to fast alone leaves pristine uncovered.

The comment on the gauntlet engine acknowledges this shape explicitly: `enrollment-validation` is listed as the state-indifferent preflight, then `pristine-lifecycle` is "the gauntlet's first content fixture" — fast members appear in gauntlet by explicit naming (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`), not by spread.

So the gauntlet-coverage question doesn't go away by adjusting fast.

Three plausible moves, ranked by how I'd weigh them:

**Option A — Hand-add to both.** Add `dockerfile-hygiene` to `ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST` (with multiline reformat per your aesthetic note) AND to `ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET` between `regime-smoke` and `hallmark-lifecycle`. Two-line docket addition, mirrors the existing hand-enumeration pattern. Scope-pure to BBABJ.

**Option B — Change pristine to run fast first.** Edit `rbq_qualify_pristine` to phase fast → gauntlet (or change gauntlet to spread fast). This makes your original belief load-bearing going forward and avoids future-fixture-author traps. Architectural; arguably belongs in its own pace or rolled into a different BB pace covering the qualify orchestrator.

**Option C — Just fast.** Accept that pristine doesn't cover this contract. Weakest — pristine is THE release gate per the paddock, and the hygiene contract is exactly the silent-first-build bug class pristine exists to catch.

I'd propose **Option A** for BBABJ scope and chivvy an itch for Option B if the trap-for-future-fixture-authors feels real. Your call?

rbfd_build() {
  zrbfd_sentinel

  local -r z_vessel_dir="${1:-}"

  # Documentation block
  buc_doc_brief "Build container image from vessel via direct builds.create submission"
  buc_doc_param "vessel_dir" "Path to vessel directory containing rbrv.env"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buc_log_args "Validate parameters"
  if test -z "${z_vessel_dir}"; then
    local z_sigils
    z_sigils=$(rbrv_list_capture) || buc_die "No vessels found"
    buc_step "Available vessels:"
    local z_sigil=""
    for z_sigil in ${z_sigils}; do
      buc_bare "        ${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/${z_sigil}"
    done
    buc_die "Vessel directory required"
  fi

  # Load and validate vessel
  zrbfc_load_vessel "${z_vessel_dir}"

  buc_log_args "Verify vessel has conjuring configuration"
  test -n "${RBRV_CONJURE_DOCKERFILE:-}" || buc_die "Vessel '${RBRV_SIGIL}' is not configured for conjuring (no RBRV_CONJURE_DOCKERFILE)"
  test -n "${RBRV_CONJURE_BLDCONTEXT:-}" || buc_die "Vessel '${RBRV_SIGIL}' is not configured for conjuring (no RBRV_CONJURE_BLDCONTEXT)"

rbfc_require_vessel_sigil() {

  local z_s=""

# available vessels and dies.  On success, writes resolved path to ZRBFC_VESSEL_RESOLVED_DIR_FILE.
zrbfc_resolve_vessel() {
  zrbfc_sentinel

  local -r z_arg="${1:-}"

  # Try as path first, then as sigil under RBRR_VESSEL_DIR
  if test -n "${z_arg}" && test -d "${z_arg}" && test -f "${z_arg}/rbrv.env"; then

  buc_step "Available vessels:"
  local z_sigil=""

  done
  if test -z "${z_arg}"; then
    buc_die "Vessel argument required (sigil or path)"
  fi
  buc_die "Vessel not found: ${z_arg}"
}

content.text:
**Q1**: Answer, not question. To confirm: `zrbfh_check_vessel` will use `zrbfc_resolve_vessel`; no separate list-and-die wiring.

**Q2 — explode-or-keep recommendation:**

Three factors that should drive this:

**1. Token cost is neutral.** CLAUDE.md unconditionally `@`-includes the kit context files. Moving the table from CLAUDE.md to per-kit files relocates the same payload — no savings.

**2. Friction analysis is asymmetric.**
- **Keeping unified**: adding an rbk module means editing CLAUDE.md (one file outside `Tools/rbk/`) plus the actual module. Cross-kit boundary crossed.
- **Exploding**: adding an rbk module means editing `Tools/rbk/rbk-claude-acronyms.md` (or similar new file) plus the module. All edits stay within `Tools/rbk/`. Blast radius is domain-local.

The friction is real — when you're heads-down on rbk work, popping to CLAUDE.md to maintain an acronym entry is a context switch.

**3. The "master index" argument cuts both ways.**
- **For unified**: the Project Prefix Registry and Prefix Naming Discipline already live in CLAUDE.md and they need a global view (terminal exclusivity, collision checks). Co-locating the per-module breakdown with those rules keeps "the rules for naming" and "the names" together.
- **For exploding**: but the per-module breakdown isn't actually USED by the discipline rules — those operate at the prefix level (RBFH, RBOB, RBJS — terminal prefixes). The per-module file listing (RBFH → `rbfh_FoundryHygiene.sh`) is reference, not rule. The two layers genuinely separate.

**Recommendation: explode, but cheaply.**

Specifically:
- Keep in CLAUDE.md: Project Prefix Registry, Prefix Naming Discipline, Heredoc Discipline, Linked Term conventions — the **rules and the top-level prefix→project map**.
- Move to kit-*.md files: the **per-module file mappings** (the long bulleted lists currently dominating CLAUDE.md's first 200 lines).
- Each kit gets a `### {Kit} File Acronyms` subsection in its existing `*-claude-context.md`, replacing/extending whatever's already there.

Why this is cheap:
- Some kits already do this (cmk-claude-context.md already lists MCM/AXLA/AXMCM, vvk-claude-context.md lists its few entries).
- rbk is the awkward outlier — its acronym table is massive AND mostly in CLAUDE.md while its conceptual context is in `rbk-claude-tabtarget-context.md`.
- The natural fix is a new `Tools/rbk/rbk-claude-acronyms.md` (or fold into the tabtarget file if you want fewer files).

**Caveat**: not for this pace. BBABJ should follow existing convention (edit CLAUDE.md). An "explode the acronym table" itch would be its own ₣BB pace or a sibling itch heat — touches every kit and wants its own scoped change.

Want me to chivvy that as an itch in jji_itch.md?

# jjezs_slate claude-md-acronym-table-explode

## Character

Mechanical: relocate per-module acronym mappings from project-root `CLAUDE.md` into the existing per-kit `*-claude-context.md` files. Each kit already has a context file `@`-included from CLAUDE.md, so payload is token-neutral — content moves, not duplicates. Blast radius for future per-module acronym mints collapses to domain-local.

## Move

Per-module acronym tables migrate to per-kit context files:

- **RBK** acronyms (~140 lines under `### Tools Directory` / `#### RBK Subdirectory` in CLAUDE.md) → fold into `Tools/rbk/rbk-claude-tabtarget-context.md` as a new top section, OR mint `Tools/rbk/rbk-claude-acronyms.md` if the tabtarget file feels overloaded (mount-time call).
- **BUK** acronyms → `Tools/buk/buk-claude-context.md` (which already has "BUK Concepts").
- **JJK** acronyms → `Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/jjk-claude-context.md` (already has "Job Jockey Configuration").
- **CMK** acronyms → `Tools/cmk/vov_veiled/cmk-claude-context.md` (already partially holds CMK file acronyms; consolidate, remove duplicates from CLAUDE.md).
- **GAD** acronyms → CLAUDE.md currently redirects to `Tools/gad/CLAUDE.md`; verify target completeness, remove redirect stub from project-root.
- **VOK** acronyms (RCG, VLS, VOS0) → fold into VOK context file (verify path at mount-time — context above mentions VOK but not its claude-context location).
- **CNMP Lenses** acronyms → fold into the appropriate context file in `../cnmp_CellNodeMessagePrototype/`, or leave if cross-repo coordination required (mount-time call — likely defer).

## Keep in CLAUDE.md

- Directory Permissions
- Project Prefix Registry (top-level prefix→project map)
- Prefix Naming Discipline, Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline, Minting Workflow, Kit Infrastructure Suffixes
- Heredoc Delimiter Selection
- AsciiDoc Linked Terms guidance
- Rust Build Discipline, Test Execution
- Working Preferences
- Common Workflows
- Design Principles (Load-Bearing Complexity)
- `@`-includes to kit context files (unchanged)

The split is: **rules and top-level prefix→project map stay in CLAUDE.md; per-module file listings live with the kit they describe**.

## Verification

- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` green (handbook-render re-renders full handbook through includes — surfaces broken cross-references).
- Spot-check three acronym lookups (`RBFH`, `BUC`, `MCM`) — each finds its entry in exactly one place across the loaded context.
- Project Prefix Registry in CLAUDE.md still enumerates every top-level prefix (`rb`, `bu`, `jj`, `mcm`/`axl`, `gad`, etc.).
- CLAUDE.md line-count drops materially (current acronym table dominates the file).

## Out of scope

- Adding new acronyms not currently in CLAUDE.md.
- Restructuring kit context files beyond accommodating the moved tables.
- Touching the GAD/CMK redirect entries if those already work end-to-end (verify-don't-fix).
- Cross-repo CNMP coordination if it requires touching a sibling repository.

## Silks

`claude-md-acronym-table-explode` — "explode" chosen over "split"/"decompose" because the work is fan-out across many kit files, not bisection. If RBK ends up minting its own dedicated acronyms file rather than folding into tabtarget context, the silks still fits.

  - `rbfh_check_vessel <vessel-sigil-or-path>` — calls `zrbfc_resolve_vessel` (which internally lists-and-dies on missing/invalid arg at `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh:166-195`), sources resolved vessel's `rbrv.env`, asserts conjure-mode (refuses bind/graft with operator-actionable message), runs check on `RBRV_CONJURE_DOCKERFILE`. No separate list-and-die handling required — `zrbfc_resolve_vessel` fully encapsulates that behavior.
- Tabtargets (both param1 channel; lowercase `h` since hygiene is read-only/local, paralleling `rbw-fpf`/`rbw-fpc`):
  - `tt/rbw-fhc.HygieneCheck.sh` → `rbfh_check`
  - `tt/rbw-fhv.HygieneCheckVessel.sh` → `rbfh_check_vessel`
- Zipper entries in `rbz_zipper.sh`: `RBZ_HYGIENE_CHECK` and `RBZ_HYGIENE_CHECK_VESSEL`.

## Fixture

- New theurge fixture `dockerfile-hygiene` defined in `rbtdrf_fast.rs`, registered in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`.
- **Suite membership** — Option A from mount-time gauntlet-coverage analysis:
  - Add `"dockerfile-hygiene"` to `ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST` at `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh:48`. Reformat `ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST` to multiline style matching `ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET` (one fixture per line).   - Add `"dockerfile-hygiene"` explicitly to `ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET` between `"regime-smoke"` and `"hallmark-lifecycle"` (post-§3 slot — state-indifferent at the level of synthetic cases, but the all-vessels case needs `${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}` populated). Gauntlet hand-enumerates its fast members (per `rbte_engine.sh` comment) — new fast fixtures do NOT propagate automatically; this addition is load-bearing for pristine-tier regression coverage.

- Handbook prose for the new tabtargets — operators discover via `ls tt/` plus context. Chivvy a doc itch if drift becomes noticeable.
- Spec subdoc edits to RBSFH for the test surface — fixture is implementation detail, not contract.

Current proposal `rbfh-cli-and-theurge-fixture` fits if `cli` and `fixture` both feel load-bearing at mount time. If just one (probably the fixture) ends up doing most of the work, consider relabeling.

Reslate landed. ₢BBABK cantled. Now the pre-implementation concerns:

**1. Silent-zero-iteration risk in `dh_all_vessels_pass`** — if the bash filter on `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE` has a typo, the case iterates zero vessels and passes trivially while covering nothing. Mitigation: end-of-case count assertion (≥1 conjure vessel iterated, else fail with diagnostic). Will add inline during implementation; not in docket prose.

**2. Verdict expression for negative cases** — 3 of 8 synthetic cases are negative (`dh_reject_*`). Need to find how `rbtdrf_run_tt` expresses expect-fail (existing pattern in `rbtdrf_fast.rs`). Mount-time grep, no risk; flagging because it's the one CLI-shape detail I haven't verified.

**3. BBABI carryover, not BBABJ new gap** — the conjure-path hygiene gate remains empirically unverified (live Cloud Build cost). BBABJ closes the regression gap via fast-tier coverage only; the all-vessels case exercises `rbfh_dockerfile_check` against every conjure-vessel Dockerfile, but not via the conjure path itself. Acceptable scope boundary; flagging for transparency.

**4. Question: notch shape** — three plausible breaks:
- **(a)** Single notch: CLI + fixture + suite wiring + reformat + CLAUDE.md + tabtarget context regen, one commit.
- **(b)** Two notches: ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST reformat (mechanical, no behavior change) → then everything else (CLI + fixture + wiring + docs).
- **(c)** Three notches: reformat → CLI alone → fixture + suite wiring.

I lean (a) — the reformat exists only to accommodate the new entry, so it's tightly coupled in intent. (b) is defensible if you want a clean review separation. (c) feels overdecomposed for this scope.

Which would you like?

aiTitle: Verify test integration in CI pipeline

content.text:
Both investigations done. Findings:

**#1 — All-vessels case shape (inherited pattern):**

Looking at `rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels` at `rbtdrf_fast.rs:967-1007`, the existing all-vessels case is a bash script generated as a Rust `format!` string:

```
set -euo pipefail
[source modules + kindle chain]
for z_d in "${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}"/*; do
  test -d "$z_d" || continue
  test -f "$z_d/rbrv.env" || continue
  (
    source "$z_d/rbrv.env"
    zrbrv_kindle
    zrbrv_enforce
  )
done
```

Then verdict is `Pass` on exit 0, `Fail(code)` otherwise (lines 1000-1006). **The existing pattern has the silent-zero-iteration property already** — if the directory is empty, the loop runs zero times and the case passes trivially. Our case inherits this AND adds a mode filter (`RBRV_VESSEL_MODE == conjure`), widening the silent-pass exposure (typo in the filter, all vessels in bind/graft mode, etc.).

Mitigation is a counter inside the loop plus an end-of-script assertion:

```
z_count=0
for z_d in "${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}"/*; do
  ...
  z_mode=$(set -e; source "$z_d/rbrv.env"; echo "${RBRV_VESSEL_MODE:-}")
  test "$z_mode" = "conjure" || continue
  ./tt/rbw-fhv.HygieneCheckVessel.sh "$(basename "$z_d")" || exit 1
  z_count=$((z_count + 1))
done
test "$z_count" -gt 0 || { echo "zero conjure vessels iterated" >&2; exit 1; }
```

~5 extra lines vs the inherited pattern. Mid-execution add; no replan needed.

**#2 — Verdict expression for negative cases — replan needed:**

`rbtdrf_run_tt` at `rbtdrf_fast.rs:174-202` only supports success:

```rust
if code != 0 {
    return Err(format!("{}: {} exited {} — {}", ...));
}
Ok(())
```

No `expect_ok` parameter (compare `rbtdrf_run_rv:125-171` which has it). All 7 existing call sites pass success-only semantics. Our 3 negative cases (`dh_reject_*`) need to express expect-fail for the tabtarget.

Three viable shapes:

**(a) Invert Ok/Err at call sites** — minimal-scope, but `rbtdrf_run_tt` returns `Err` on BOTH "tabtarget not found" and "tabtarget exited non-zero". A negative case would silently pass if the tabtarget couldn't be invoked. Leaky abstraction in a regression-protection fixture is bad shape.

**(b) Add `expect_ok: bool` parameter to `rbtdrf_run_tt`** — generalizes the helper. Touches 7 existing call sites (add `true` arg). Clean contract but scope ripple.

**(c) Mint sibling `rbtdrf_run_tt_neg`** — distinct helper with three-outcome distinction: invocation-error → `Err`, exit-0 → `Err("expected failure")`, exit-nonzero → `Ok`. Zero churn at existing call sites, only the 3 negative-case callsites use it.

I'd recommend **(c)**. Cleanest scope boundary — existing helper untouched, new helper used only where it's needed, no leaky abstraction.

**Replan implications**: this isn't in the BBABJ docket currently. Two paths:

**#1 — Framework status:**

Not obsolete, but pattern-debt. The "bash test framework" you're seeing is actually Rust fixtures that generate bash scripts as `format!` strings and shell out. It exists because most SUTs (rbrr.env, rbrv.env, tabtargets) are bash — sourcing and kindling can only happen in a shell. So *some* bash is unavoidable.

But the **iteration logic** in `rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels` is in the bash side purely by historical accident — Rust could read the vessel directory, parse each rbrv.env for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`, filter, and invoke the tabtarget per-vessel via `rbtdrf_run_tt`. Same authoritative bash-sourcing for the env files (one-shot per vessel for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`), but the loop, counter, and per-vessel error attribution live in compiled code.

That move gives us:
- No silent-zero-iteration risk (count is an explicit Rust variable)
- Per-vessel stdout/stderr files (free from `rbtdrf_run_tt`'s logging)
- Clean per-vessel error messages
- A pattern the next person can copy

I'd recommend: **write our new case Rust-led** (iteration in Rust, env sourcing one-shot bash per vessel), and **chivvy an itch** to retrofit the existing `rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels` and `rbtdrf_rv_rbrn_all_nameplates` to the same shape. That keeps BBABJ scope-tight while not propagating the bad pattern.

**#2 — Simple version:**

The existing Rust helper that invokes tabtargets only knows how to verify the tabtarget succeeded. We have 3 negative cases that need to verify the tabtarget *failed* (because `rbfh_dockerfile_check` aborts on bad Dockerfiles). The helper doesn't express that.

Cheapest fix: mint a small sibling helper (~20 lines Rust) used only by the negative cases — distinguishes "tabtarget couldn't run" (real error) from "tabtarget ran and failed" (the success condition for negative tests). Zero churn at existing call sites.

I have a meaningful finding. Here's the established pattern I missed.

**Theurge ALREADY tests library functions without going through tabtargets** — the 47 enrollment-validation cases and 27 regime-validation cases all source `.sh` modules directly and call public functions (`buv_vet`, `zbuv_kindle`, `zrbrv_enforce`, etc.). They use `rbtdrf_run_bash` — generate a bash script from Rust, the script sources the module and calls the function, capture exit code, assert against `expect_ok: bool`. No tabtargets in this path.

`rbtdrf_run_tt` is for testing the **operator surface** specifically — when the SUT *is* a user-facing tabtarget (kludge, ordain, render commands). The principle documented at `rbtdri_invocation.rs:19-20` ("theurge invokes bottle operations exclusively through tabtargets") applies to operator-shaped operations, not library-function tests.

**This collapses the problem.**

For BBABJ, the test target isn't the tabtarget — it's `rbfh_dockerfile_check`, a public library function in `rbfh_FoundryHygiene.sh`. Theurge can source the module and call the function directly, exactly mirroring the `rbtdrf_run_rv` shape:

```
script = "source rbfh_FoundryHygiene.sh; zrbfh_kindle; rbfh_dockerfile_check <tempfile>"
positive case: expect_ok = true
negative case: expect_ok = false  
```

The `expect_ok: bool` mechanism already exists in `rbtdrf_run_rv` and `rbtdrf_run_ev`. Both positive and negative cases use the same plumbing. **No new helper, no tabtarget invocation in the test path, no naming convention needed.**

The user-facing tabtargets `rbw-fhc`/`rbw-fhv` still get minted (operator ad-hoc hygiene checks) but theurge bypasses them.

**Recommendations**:

1. **BBABJ test path**: source `rbfh_FoundryHygiene.sh`, call `rbfh_dockerfile_check` directly via `rbtdrf_run_bash` with `expect_ok`. Mirror `rbtdrf_run_rv` shape. The 8 synthetic cases become trivial.

2. **All-vessels case**: same pattern — Rust loop over `read_dir(RBRR_VESSEL_DIR)`, one-shot bash per vessel for mode-source + hygiene call (or fully Rust if rbrv.env parsing is cheap; .env line-format is trivial). Counter at end.

3. **General principle to reaffirm in the docket** (if you want it captured for the next person): theurge tests library functions by direct source + call. Tabtargets are operator-ergonomics layer, not test interface. Already the dominant pattern in `rbtdrf_fast.rs`; we follow it rather than expanding the tabtarget path.

4. **Test-only entry points in `*_cli.sh`**: not needed. Library functions like `rbfh_dockerfile_check` already at right granularity. If a future case needs to test a sub-phase of a complex workflow function (e.g., "just the slot-resolution part of kludge"), revisit then. YAGNI until a real case forces it.

5. **Naming convention**: not needed because (4). If the pattern ever DOES earn its keep, `<prefix>_t_<name>` (theurge-target) would be the natural fit.

The operator constraint (no new tabtargets for testing) is automatically satisfied by following the established pattern. Reslate BBABJ to drop tabtarget invocation from the test path?

You're right. Let me assess the mess directly.

**Mess from current theurge-bypasses-tabtargets pattern:**

In `rbtdrf_fast.rs` enrollment/regime cases, theurge sources `.sh` modules directly and calls functions like `zrbcc_kindle`, `zrbrr_kindle`, `zrbrv_enforce`. This creates:

- **Path coupling** — theurge encodes `Tools/rbk/rbrr_regime.sh`, `Tools/rbk/rbgc_Constants.sh`, etc. as string literals across many `format!` blocks. Moving a module breaks theurge in 10+ places silently.
- **Kindle-chain coupling** — theurge replicates the source-and-kindle ordering that ALSO lives in every `*_cli.sh`. BBABI's `zrbfh_kindle` addition required updating multiple cli furnishings; if any theurge case needed rbfh modules it'd require a parallel update. Two sources of truth for "how do you wake up this module."
- **Private-function reach** — `z`-prefix means private by BCG convention, yet theurge routinely calls `z*_kindle`. The convention has an asterisk: "private except for theurge tests." Not formally declared anywhere.
- **Refactor risk** — any internal restructure of a module risks breaking tests that reach inside. The "what's testable from outside" surface is implicit, defined by accident of which functions happen to be public-named.

**Mess from no controlled secondary interface:**

There's no declared "test API" layer. When theurge wants to exercise a sub-workflow that doesn't have a natural tabtarget, the only options are:
- Reach deeper into internals (worsens M1-M4 above)
- Add a test-only tabtarget (pollutes operator surface — your stated concern)
- Add a public function to the cli that nothing else uses (clutters library API)

None are clean. The result is theurge ends up with the "reach into internals" choice by default.

**Verdict for BBABJ:**

You're right — go through the tabtargets we're already minting. `rbw-fhc <path>` and `rbw-fhv <sigil>` ARE the contract surface; tests should exercise that surface, not bypass it. Cost: the small `rbtdrf_run_tt_neg` helper (~20 lines Rust) for expect-fail cases. Cost is bounded and one-time.

Benefits: theurge knows colophons, not source paths or kindle chains. Refactoring `rbfh_FoundryHygiene.sh` internals — function renames, kindle restructure, source-path moves — leaves the tests untouched as long as `rbw-fhc`/`rbw-fhv` external behavior holds. Mirrors the principle documented at `rbtdri_invocation.rs:19-20`.

**Verdict on the secondary-interface idea (test entry points in `*_cli.sh`):**

Not needed for BBABJ. The two tabtargets cover what we test. Worth noting as a pattern in reserve if/when a future pace surfaces a real sub-workflow that needs testing without an obvious tabtarget — at that point a `<prefix>_t_<op>` convention would be the natural answer, and it'd land alongside that pace's concrete need rather than as speculative scaffolding now.

**Chivvy worth raising**: the existing 74 enrollment/regime cases bypass tabtargets via direct source-and-call. That's the legacy mess. Migrating those is its own substantial effort and not in BBABJ scope, but worth an itch entry: "theurge bypass-of-tabtargets in enrollment/regime cases — migration to contract-surface testing."

# jjezs_slate theurge-contract-surface-migration

## Character

Design conversation followed by careful migration. Theurge's enrollment-validation (~47 cases) and regime-validation (~27 cases) in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs` currently bypass tabtargets and exercise modules via direct source-and-call — encoding source paths, replicating kindle chains, and reaching into `z`-prefixed internals. Identified during BBABJ as legacy mess; this pace surveys, categorizes, and repairs.

Don't dictate repair shape at slate-time. Study first, let analysis inform the repair plan, execute. May decompose into multiple cantled paces if the work breaks naturally into independent migrations.

## Scope

- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs` — primary surface, 74+ cases in enrollment-validation and regime-validation families plus the two all-* cases (`rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels`, `rbtdrf_rv_rbrn_all_nameplates`).
- Sibling files in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/` following the same bypass pattern — verify at mount-time which apply: `rbtdrk_canonical.rs`, `rbtdro_onboarding.rs`, `rbtdrp_pristine.rs`, `rbtdrf_handbook.rs`.
- Engine helpers (`rbtdrf_run_ev`, `rbtdrf_run_rv`, `rbtdrf_run_bash`) — touched only if repair shape demands.

## Principles (from BBABJ design conversation — context for future minter)

- **Theurge tests should exercise contract surfaces, not module internals.** Where an operator tabtarget covers the operation, theurge invokes the tabtarget via `rbtdrf_run_tt` (or `rbtdrf_run_tt_neg` for expect-fail, minted in `₢BBABJ`).
- **No new tabtargets purely for testing.** Tabtargets are operator surface; adding test-only colophons pollutes that surface.
- **For sub-workflows or pure-library tests without natural tabtarget coverage**: declared test entry points in the relevant `*_cli.sh` module are preferable to reaching into `z`-internals. Naming sketch (not locked): `<prefix>_t_<op>` (`_t_` for theurge-target). Convention locks at first concrete use.
- **The mess being repaired**:
  - **Path coupling** — theurge encodes module source paths (`Tools/rbk/rbrr_regime.sh`, etc.) as string literals across many `format!` blocks. Module moves break theurge silently.
  - **Kindle-chain coupling** — theurge replicates source-and-kindle ordering that also lives in every `*_cli.sh`. Two sources of truth for module wakeup; BBABI's `zrbfh_kindle` addition demonstrated the drift surface.
  - **Private-function reach** — `z`-prefixed functions are conventionally private, yet theurge routinely calls `z*_kindle`. The convention has an undocumented exception ("private except for theurge tests"); the public/private boundary is fuzzy.
  - **Refactor risk** — internal restructure can break tests that reach inside; "what's testable from outside" is implicit, defined by accident of which functions happen to be public-named.

## Suggested approach (mount-time judgment)

1. **Survey**: categorize each in-scope case into one of:
   - **Tabtarget-coverable** — existing operator tabtarget serves as the test surface; migration is mechanical (source-and-call → `rbtdrf_run_tt`).
   - **Test-API-required** — case tests something without natural tabtarget coverage; needs a declared `*_t_*` entry point in a cli or library module.
   - **Pure-utility** — case tests a self-contained library function (e.g., BUV type checks via `buv_vet`) where direct sourcing of a public function is the cleanest shape; the mess is bounded and migration may not earn its keep.
2. **Plan**: decide whether to execute migration inline or decompose into cantled paces by category. Cantle if categories suggest independent effort sizes.
3. **Execute**: preserve case behavior — verdicts before and after must match across all 74+ cases.

## Verification

- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` green at completion (case count preserved; verdicts match pre-migration baseline).
- Spot-check refactor safety claim: temporarily move or rename a module file, confirm theurge still passes (or fails with a contract-level diagnostic, not a "no such file" error).
- If new `*_t_*` entry points minted: their addition is reflected in CLAUDE.md acronym mappings (post-BBABK relocation, this may live in per-kit context files).

## Out of scope

- Adding new theurge cases. Migration-only.
- Bash testbenches in `Tools/buk/` (separate codepath, separate concern).
- User-facing tabtarget changes — no new ones, existing ones unchanged.
- Locking `*_t_*` as a universal naming policy — the first concrete use locks it.
- Engine-helper redesign beyond what migration demands.

## References

- ₢BBABJ design conversation — context for the mess characterization and proposed principles.
- `rbtdri_invocation.rs:19-20` — documented principle "theurge invokes bottle operations exclusively through tabtargets" (currently observed only for crucible-shaped ops; this pace extends to enrollment/regime).

## Silks

`theurge-contract-surface-migration` — destination shape. The work is "migrating theurge testing to contract surfaces (tabtargets where natural, declared test APIs otherwise)" with study preceding execution.

  - `tt/rbw-fhv.HygieneCheckVessel.sh` → `rbfh_check_vessel`
- Zipper entries in `rbz_zipper.sh`: `RBZ_HYGIENE_CHECK` and `RBZ_HYGIENE_CHECK_VESSEL`.
- **New theurge helper `rbtdrf_run_tt_neg`** in `rbtdrf_fast.rs` (~20 lines): mirrors `rbtdrf_run_tt` shape but inverts success-direction — exit 0 → `Err("expected failure, got success")`, exit non-zero → `Ok(())`. Invocation-error (tabtarget not found, launcher failure) still propagates as `Err`. Used only by the 3 negative synthetic cases. Sibling helper preferred over generalizing `rbtdrf_run_tt` with `expect_ok: bool` (which would touch 7 existing call sites).

## Fixture

- New theurge fixture `dockerfile-hygiene` defined in `rbtdrf_fast.rs`, registered in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`.
- **Suite membership** (Option A from mount-time gauntlet-coverage analysis):
  - Add `"dockerfile-hygiene"` to `ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST` at `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh:48`. Reformat `ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST` to multiline style matching `ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET` (one fixture per line). The `"${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}"` spread in `ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE`/`_CRUCIBLE`/`_COMPLETE` propagates transitively — fixture flows into all three for free.
  - Add `"dockerfile-hygiene"` explicitly to `ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET` between `"regime-smoke"` and `"hallmark-lifecycle"` (post-§3 slot; the all-vessels case needs `${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}` populated). Gauntlet hand-enumerates fast members — load-bearing addition for pristine-tier regression coverage.
- **Synthetic cases (8)**: Dockerfile bodies as inline Rust `&'static str` constants. Each case writes the body to a temp file in the case's `dir`, invokes `tt/rbw-fhc <tempfile>` via either `rbtdrf_run_tt` (positive) or `rbtdrf_run_tt_neg` (negative), maps the helper's `Result` to `rbtdre_Verdict`. Coverage:
  - `dh_accept_parameterized` (positive — `FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_1}`)
  - `dh_accept_scratch` (positive — `FROM scratch`)
  - `dh_accept_multistage_as` (positive — `FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_1} AS builder` coda admissible)
  - `dh_accept_empty` (positive — empty file passes silently)
  - `dh_accept_comments_only` (positive — column-0 `#` lines only)
  - `dh_reject_hardcoded_literal` (negative — `FROM python:3.12-slim`)
  - `dh_reject_tab_in_from` (negative — tab anywhere on FROM line)
  - `dh_reject_trailing_backslash` (negative — line ends with `\`)
- **All-vessels case `dh_all_vessels_pass`** (Rust-led, no bash format-string loop): Rust loop over `read_dir(RBRR_VESSEL_DIR)`. `RBRR_VESSEL_DIR` resolved once via a small one-shot bash that sources rbrr.env + echoes the value (kindle ceremony is unavoidable for derived-path resolution). For each vessel directory: one-shot bash sources `<vessel>/rbrv.env` and echoes `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; if `conjure`, invoke `tt/rbw-fhv <sigil>` via `rbtdrf_run_tt`. Theurge knows colophons and sigils; not source paths or kindle chains.

## Touches

- CLAUDE.md File Acronym Mappings: extend the existing RBFH entry with a parenthetical mention of the CLI partner (`rbfh_cli.sh`). One acronym per module-family — mirrors RBOB pattern (`rbob_bottle.sh` listed; `rbob_cli.sh` not separately).
- Regenerate `Tools/rbk/rbk-claude-tabtarget-context.md` via `tt/rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh` so the two new tabtargets appear in the Foundry table.
- No spec edits required: `RBSFH-dockerfile_hygiene.adoc` describes the rule, not the test surface. `RBSAK`/`RBSAC` references to `{rbsfh_dockerfile_hygiene}` already landed in `₢BBABI`.

## Verification

- `bash -n` clean on `rbfh_cli.sh`, `rbte_engine.sh`, and any other touched bash modules.
- `cargo build` clean in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/` (the helper mint + fixture registration are Rust changes).
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` green (case count grows ~98 → ~107).
- Confirm `dockerfile-hygiene` listed in `ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET` by inspection (running gauntlet end-to-end is cost-prohibitive at notch-time; membership-by-inspection is sufficient).
- Sanity-check: temporarily flip one synthetic case's positive/negative expectation, confirm the fixture reports the case as failed (verdict assertion is wired correctly), then revert.

## Out of scope

- Handbook prose for the new tabtargets — operators discover via `ls tt/` plus context. Chivvy a doc itch if drift becomes noticeable.
- Spec subdoc edits to RBSFH for the test surface — fixture is implementation detail, not contract.
- Decomposing `rbfh_check_vessel`'s no-arg behavior into a separate `rbfh_list_vessels` function — premature factoring; `zrbfc_resolve_vessel` already encapsulates list-and-die.
- CLAUDE.md acronym-table relocation — chivvied as cantled pace `claude-md-acronym-table-explode` (`₢BBABK`).
- Migrating the existing 74 enrollment/regime cases off direct-sourcing — chivvied as `₢BJAAB` (theurge-contract-surface-migration) in ₣BJ.
- Architectural change to make `rbq_qualify_pristine` run fast → gauntlet rather than just gauntlet — out of pace scope; the explicit gauntlet hand-add covers BBABJ's regression-coverage purpose.
- Generalizing `rbtdrf_run_tt` to take `expect_ok: bool` (touching 7 existing call sites) — the sibling-helper approach scope-isolates to the 3 new negative-case callsites.

## Silks

Current proposal `rbfh-cli-and-theurge-fixture` fits — CLI mints two functions + two tabtargets + zipper + helper; fixture adds 9 cases. Both halves load-bearing. Keep unless implementation reveals one collapsing.
